coredata.dc:title: Application of green-synthesized carbon dots for imaging of cancerous cell lines and detection of anthraquinone drugs using silica-coated CdTe quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadinejad, Arash
Abnous, Khalil
Alinezhad Nameghi, Morteza
Yahyazadeh, Roghayeh
Hamrah, Sara
Senobari, Fereshteh
Mohajeri, Seyed Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Chemotherapy drugs of daunorubicin and doxorubicin treat cancers with many side effects. So, detection of them in the biological system for regulation and controlling of usage is essential. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent method was introduced for detection of daunorubicin and doxorubicin using bell pepper-based carbon dots, as the variable signal, and silica-coated CdTe quantum dots, as the constant signal. The detection was done based on variations of carbon dots intensity in the presence of drugs in comparison with the constant intensity of silica-coated CdTe quantum dots. The proposed ratiometric fluorescent method was successfully used for detection of daunorubicin and doxorubicin range of 54.37–13594.34 nmolL−1 and 86.2–17242 nmolL−1, with a detection limit of 18.53 nmolL−1 and 29 nmolL−1, respectively. Also, this method was used for detection of drugs in serum samples with recovery ranges of 86.14–99.62 (RSD 3–1.47%) and 86.32–97.53 (3.38–1.48%), respectively. Finally, after evaluation of carbon dots toxicity by MTT test, carbon dots was applied for imaging of prostate cancer cell lines (PC-3) and breast cancer cell lines (MCF7). The results demonstrated that despite improvement of the repeatability and interferences reduction by ratiometric method, also carbon dots were successfully applied for imaging of cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CdTe quantum dots
Daunorubicin
Doxorubicin
Ratiometric method
Imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study supported by Vice Chancellor of Research of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran, through the grant numbers 971785 and 971760.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs by (A) UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Inset: CDs solution under UV (up) and room (down) light.; (B) TEM; (C) EDX; (D) FTIR; (E) DLS; (F) XRD.
Charactrization of Si@CdTe QDs by: (A) Fluorescence spectra of MSA-CdTe QDs and Si@CdTe QDs (λexc = 360 nm); (B) UV light radiation: left: MSA-capped CdTe QDs, Right: Si@CdTe QDs; (C) EDX; (D) FT-IR.

(A-C) Optimization of conditions: (A) Concentration of CDs; (B) Incubation time; and (C) PH; (D) Fluorescence spectra of RF system in presence of different concentrations of DOX (up to down: 0, 86.2, 155.17, 172.42, 344.84, 862.1, 1724.2, 3448.39, 5172.59, 6896.79, 8620.99, 12069.4, 17,242 nmolL−1). Inset shows CDs solution under UV light (360 nm) in the absence (left) and presence (right) of DOX (17242 nmolL−1) (E) Calibration curve of DOX detection (86.2–17242 nmolL−1); (F) Fluorescence spectra of RF system in presence of different concentrations of DAN (up to down: 0, 54.37, 108.75, 543.77, 1087.5, 1631.3, 2718.8, 3806.4, 5437.3, 8156.6, 10875.48, 13594.34 nmolL−1). Inset shows CDs solution under UV light (360 nm) in the absence (left) and presence (right) of DAN (13594.34 nmolL−1); (G) Calibration curve of DAN detection (54.37–13594.34 nmolL−1). Each point represents as mean ± SD (n = 3) (H) Overlaping of absorbance spectra of DOX and DAN with fluorescence spectra of RF system (λex= 360 nm).

(A,B) Interference study for detection of: (A) DOX, and (B) DAN. Responses for interferers are presented as: Signal response in presence of interfere- Signal response in absence of interfere.; (C, D) Results of MTT assay for evaluation of toxicity of CDs on: (C) MCF-7, and (D) PC3 cell lines. Each point represents as mean ± SD (n = 4). (E, F) Fluorescence imaging of: (E) MCF-7, (F) PC3 cell lines (λex = 466 nm).
Synthesis protocol of: (A) CDs, and (B) Si@CdTe QDs; (C) application of CDs in ratiometric detection and imaging.
Comparison of introduced RF sensor in this study with other sensors.

row.entry: multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified platinum electrode (Pt/MWCNTs)
carbon paste electrode modified with ZnO nanoparticle/1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (ZnO-NPs/BMTFB/CPE)
nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) suspended in chitosan-modified gold electrode
molecularly imprinted polymer layer on the carbon dot-modified silica surface
Bell pepper-based CDs and Si@CdTe QDs for ratiometric detection
gold nanoparticles-multiwalled nanotubes-modified glassy carbon electrode (Au-MCNTs/ GCE)
3D gold brush nanoelectrode ensembles (BNEEs) containing gold nanowires
an 808 nm-excited upconversion fuorescent sensor (CA-coated NaYF4:Yb/Er/Nd@NaYF4:Nd)
Bell pepper-based CDs and Si@CdTe QDs for ratiometric detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The result of DOX and DAN measurement in the serum matrix.

head.ce:title: Application of green-synthesized carbon dots for imaging of cancerous cell lines and detection of anthraquinone drugs using silica-coated CdTe quantum dots-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Biotechnology and Nanotechnology, School of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Biotechnology and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medical Biotechnology and Nanotechnology, School of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacodynamics and Toxicology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacodynamics and Toxicology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemotherapy drugs of daunorubicin and doxorubicin treat cancers with many side effects. So, detection of them in the biological system for regulation and controlling of usage is essential. In this study, a ratiometric fluorescent method was introduced for detection of daunorubicin and doxorubicin using bell pepper-based carbon dots, as the variable signal, and silica-coated CdTe quantum dots, as the constant signal. The detection was done based on variations of carbon dots intensity in the presence of drugs in comparison with the constant intensity of silica-coated CdTe quantum dots. The proposed ratiometric fluorescent method was successfully used for detection of daunorubicin and doxorubicin range of 54.37–13594.34 nmolL−1 and 86.2–17242 nmolL−1, with a detection limit of 18.53 nmolL−1 and 29 nmolL−1, respectively. Also, this method was used for detection of drugs in serum samples with recovery ranges of 86.14–99.62 (RSD 3–1.47%) and 86.32–97.53 (3.38–1.48%), respectively. Finally, after evaluation of carbon dots toxicity by MTT test, carbon dots was applied for imaging of prostate cancer cell lines (PC-3) and breast cancer cell lines (MCF7). The results demonstrated that despite improvement of the repeatability and interferences reduction by ratiometric method, also carbon dots were successfully applied for imaging of cell lines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new and fascinating class of fluorescent nanomaterial with a size below 10 nm, have recently gained considerable attention of scientists due to their unique properties in comparison with other traditional quantum dots. CDs possess different advantages including low toxicity, biocompatibility, high photostability, high solubility in water, eco-friendly, and strong fluorescence intensity [1,2]. Heretofore, a wide range of methods including plasma [3], laser ablation [4], arc discharge [5], electrochemical [6], hydrothermal [7], acidic oxidation [8], ultrasonic [9] and microwave treatment [10] have been reported for synthesis of CDs. Recently, hydrothermal method using natural resource materials as precursors has been developed for preparation of CDs. In this context, several natural resources such as oranges [11], potatoes [12], hair [13], watermelon peels [14], bananas [15], milk [16], coffee [17], honey [18], ginger juice [19], orange waste peels [20], and pomegranate fruit [21] have been employed for development of a simple, low cost, and green synthesis method.

Doxorubicin (DOX) and daunorubicin (DAN), known as anthracycline anticancer antibiotics, are widely applied to treat different kinds of cancers such as lungs, breast, lymphocytic leukemia, ovarian, acute, bladder, prostate, and also neoplastic disease [22–24]. Nonetheless, utilizing these drugs at the concentrations above-allowed level in blood may lead to tissue necrosis, marrow suppression, cardiac toxicity, and oral ulcers [23,25]. The normal level in blood after usage of free dugs and liposomal encapsulation is 1–2 µgmL−1 and 40 µgmL−1, respectively [26]. So, due to the toxicity effects of these drugs, the development of sensitive and simple analytical techniques is of great importance to clinical research. In recent years, several methods such as liquid chromatography [27], electrochemistry [28], spectrophotometry [29], fluorescence [24], Raman scattering [30], and resonance Rayleigh scattering [31] have been employed for quantification of anthracycline antibiotics.

Among the aforementioned methods, single wavelength fluorescence sensors, as the subset of the fluorescence method, have been of great interest in analytical methods for anthracycline drugs [24,25,32] due to their advantages, such as simplicity and sensitivity. Unfortunately, some analytical errors arising from inconstancy in concentration, environment, and excitation source have limited their usage [33,34]. Ratiometric fluorescent (RF) sensors with a built-in correction factor can effectively remove these problems. So, RF sensors, in which emission ratios do determinations at two different wavelengths, enhance precision, accuracy, and sensitivity [35].

This research reported quantification of DOX and DAN using RF sensors designed by green synthesized CDs from green bell pepper and silica-coated CdTe quantum dots (Si@CdTe QDs) in serum samples. The quantification was done based on variations of fluorescence intensity (FI) of CDs compared to constant FI of Si@CdTe QDs. Moreover, imaging of breast cancer cell line (MCF7) and prostate cancer cell line (PC3) performed using CDs. Based on our knowledge, the applications of green synthesized CDs from green bell pepper in designing RF sensor for determination of DOX and DAN and imaging of MCF-7 and PC3 cell lines have not been reported. Moreover the application of Si@CdTe QDs as a constant and single signal for operation of the two signal RF sensor is an impressive idea to prevent effect of drugs on constant signal. The proposed method synergically possesses advantages including inexpensive and green source of carbon, one pot and simple synthesis of CDs and QDs and also simple protocol of designed RF sensor.

All experimental reagents were of analytical grade. Calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl2·2.5H2O), 3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA), Sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), ethanol absolute, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), acetonitrile (C2H3N), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), Copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), Potassium nitrate (KNO3), l-Glutamine, Potassium carbonate (K2CO3) was purchased from Merck. Doxorubicin (DOXO-cell) and Daunorubicin (Injection 20 mg) were purchased from Pfizer. Bell pepper was purchased from a local vegetable market in Mashhad. Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) containing Na2HPO4 (14.4 g), KCl (2 g) ،NaCl (80 g), KH2PO4 (2.4 g) was prepared in deionized water (1 L). Then adjustment of pH 7.4 was done by NaOH and HCl solution and prepared buffer was sterilized by autoclave.

BioTek Microplate Reader (Synergy™ H4 Hybrid Reader, USA) was used for recording UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The appearance and size of CDs were measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (LEO 912AB, 120kv, Germany). The crystalline phases of C-dots was performed by a GNR (Italy) X-ray diffractometer (XRD). LEO VP 1450 (Germany) Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) was applied to obtain energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum of nanoparticles. PerkinElmer spectrophotometer (Version 10.4.4, USA) was used to record Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the region 450–4000 cm−1. The zeta potential of samples was calculated by Malvern Panalytical dynamic light scattering (DLS) apparatus (UK). A JuLI™ fluorescence microscope (NanoEnTek Company) was used for imaging of cell lines by excitation at 466 nm.

A different source of protein [36], ascorbic acid or carbohydrate [37] has been used to synthesize the CDs via various methods. In this study, a green bell was selected to synthesize CDs duo to high-quality contents such as carotenoids, ascorbic acid, carbohydrate, and other carbonaceous organic materials, according to a typical, simple, and one-pot green synthesis method. Firstly, a few bell peppers were sliced, and after separation of seeds, fragmented to several small spices and ground by a juicer. Then obtained paste was added to 100 mL of double distilled water, and the mixture was transferred into an stainless steel autoclave reactor, tightly closed, and heated at 200℃ for 6 h. Afterward, the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, and the dark solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate large particles. Finally, the resultant was filtered by 0.45 µm and 0.22 μm syringe filter, freeze-dried, and the resulted powder, which was highly soluble in water, kept at −20℃ in the dark for later use. Then 6 mg of obtained powder was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water for the daily administration and was kept at 4 ℃.

The synthesis of MSA-capped CdTe QDs (MSA-CdTe QDs) was done according to our previous studies using a simple and one-pot method without preparation and inner pressure adjustment [38,39]. To obtain MSA-CdTe QDs, CdCl2 (20 mg,), MSA (45 mg) and Na2TeO3 (4.4 mg) was mixed with 100 mL borate–acetic acid buffer solution (PH = 7). It was performed in a one-neck flask and vigorously stirring for 10 min. Then 45 mg NaBH4 was swiftly added into reaction solution and continuously stirred for 10 min. The reaction solution was refluxed at 100℃ for 24 h under outdoor conditions. After synthesis completion, reaction solution slowly cooled in room temperature and after ethanol adding, MSA-CdTe QDs were separated by centrifugation at 8500 rpm. Finally, resultant nanoparticles were dissolved in double-distilled water (20 mL) and were kept at 4℃ under dark-condition.

To create Si@CdTe QDs, MSA-CdTe QDs (2 mL) and APTES (60 mL) were added to ethanol (3 mL) in a falcon. After stirring for 30 min, TEOS (270 mL) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) (300 mL) were added to this solution and were stirred for 24 h. The final solution was washed with methanol, and dried powder was kept under dark-condition.

In this study, the experimental process was based on emission fluctuation of CDs (in the presence of DOX and DAN) at 450 nm compared to constant emission of MSA-CdTe QDs at 550 nm, which was proportional to the concentration of the anticancer drug. The measurement was done by incubation of several concentrations of DOX and DAN, CDs (30 mgL−1), Si@CdTe QDs (12500 ppm) in the 200 μL aqueous medium after 40 min. Then emission wavelength was read by Synergy™ H4 Hybrid reader in 400–700 nm after excitation at 360 nm. The calibration curve was plotted according to the signal changes of (F0-F)/F0. F0 and F are fluorescence intensity at 450 nm in the absence and presence of the drug, respectively, which is divided on fluorescence intensity at 550 nm. The data report was as means ± SD. Relative standard deviation (RSD %) was used as a repeatability factor for four repetitions.

Blood sample of a healthy volunteer was obtained by a clinical laboratory in Mashhad. Then the serum sample was obtained by centrifuge at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Due to the high protein of serum, it was deproteinized by acetonitrile (ACN: serum 1:1) according to reference [40,41]. After 5 min shaking, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm at 4℃ for 10 min. The resultant was filtered two times. The solution was heated at 60 ℃ to acetonitrile removal, and the pellet was diluted 25 times with PBS. Different concentrations of drugs (Table 2), CDs (30 mg/L) and Si@CdTe QDs (12500 mg/L) were incubated with blood serum (200 mL) for 40 min.

PC-3 cells (human prostate cancer cell line) and MCF-7 (human breast cancer cell line) were provided from the Pasteur Institute of Iran. Cells were cultured after washing with PBS (×1) and trypsinization for each passage with RPMI 1640 containing 100 units/mL penicillin–streptomycin and 10 % heat-inactivated FBS. The culture flasks were kept in a humid atmosphere at 37 °C and 5 % CO2.

Cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay, which is based on the ability of live cells to convert tetrazolium salt MTT to formazan crystals by mitochondrial enzymes [42]. Cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay based on the ability of live cells to convert tetrazolium salt MTT to formazan crystals by mitochondrial enzymes. Briefly, cells (1 × 104) were seeded in 96-well plates; consequently, the cells were treated with different concentrations (5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 1500 mgL-1) of the CDs and incubated at 37℃ and 5 % CO2 flow for 24 h. The untreated cell was considered as the control group. After replacing the new medium in the wells, MTT (10 μL of 5 mgmL−1) was added to the wells and incubated at 37℃ in dark conditions for 4 h. Thereafter, the medium was thrown away and replaced with DMSO (100 μL). The viability of cells was checked by determining the absorption difference in each well at 570 and 630 nm.

PC-3 and MCF7 cells (5 × 104) were placed in 24-well microplate. After 24 h incubation at 37℃, the culture wells medium was replaced by an RPMI1640 without FBS (500 μL) containing different concentrations of CDs. Then microplate was incubated at condition of 37℃ and darkness for three h. Finally, after deeply wells washing with PBS, the result was analyzed by fluorescence microscopy [43].

It is suggested that the probable mechanism of fluorescent CDs production is associated with the carbonaceous natural organic contents in bell pepper. Although the mechanism of CDs formation is still vague but the possible mechanism can be related to the sequential events of dehydration, fragmentation and condensation of carbonaceous contents which may be followed by aromatization and carbonization (Scheme 1 ) [44]. The sugar content plays significant role in CDs formation. The hydrothermal condition leads to hydrolysation of sucrose to glucose and fructose. Moreover glucose can be isomerised to fructose. Afterwards, the dehydration and decomposition processes of fructose and glucose take place to form furfural compounds and some organic acids such as citric acid and ascorbic acid which are able to control dehydration and decomposition processes. In the following, resulted products polymerized and condensed to form soluble polymers which aromatized to clusters during aldol condensation, cycloaddition and hydroxymethylation of furan compounds. Finally nucleation of CDs was happened through the saturation of aromatic clusters [11].

According to the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1
A), CDs possessed a broad absorption between 300 and 400 nm which is completely comparable with literature [45]. Moreover, using the fluorimetry technique, the maximum emission intensity of CDs was recorded at 450 nm wavelength (Fig. 1 A). It is necessary to mention, as it is obviously shown under room light, the CDs solution was appeared light yellow, but under UV light, it was seen blue color (inset). Characterization and size distribution of CDs with blue emission (450 nm) was obtained via TEM. As shown in Fig. 1B, the size of synthesized CDs was estimated smaller than 5 nm. Also, in this study, EDX was used to verification of CDs ingredients. According to the pattern (Fig. 1 C), composition mainly contained carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. The functionality of the CDs surface was analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. According to Fig. 1D, the absorption bands of the functionalization group of hydroxyl viewed at 3381.3 cm−1 (υ OH) and 1073.6 cm−1 (δ OH). Also, carboxylate (υ COO–) presented absorption bands at 1603.9 cm−1 and 1407.5 cm−1 related to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations. Also, the absorption band at 2929.6 cm−1 is related to CH [46]. In addition, the zeta potential of the synthesized CDs was obtained −17.6 mv (Fig. 1E) by DLS, which proved that CDs have carbocyclic acid groups on their surface.

The crystallinity and purity of CDs structure was evaluated by XRD analysis. As it is shown in Fig. 1F, the prominent diffraction peaks were located at 2θ values of 19.72° (200), 21.90°(001), 44.49° (002), 76.39° (512) which match with the crystal plane of carbon with Entry number 01–080-5332.

In order to evaluation of quantum yield, quinine sulphate was applied as reference using following equation [47,48]:
Φ C
=
Φ R
A R
/
A C
I C
/
I R
η C
/
η R
2

Where Φ, as quantum yield, A, as absorbance at excitation wavelength (360 nm), I, as fluorescence intensity, and η, as refraction index of solvent, are main parameters of equation. Moreover the subscript letters of R and C are referred to reference and carbon dots. The quinine sulphate was dissolved in 0.1 molL−1 H2SO4 solution (η = 1.33) with quantum yield of 54. Also carbon dots were dissolved in ultrapure water (η = 1.33) and optical density was considered less than 0.1 to prevent the re-absorption event. According to the aforementioned equation (IC = 3859, IR = 12404, AR and AC at 360 nm = 0.04) quantum yield of synthesized CDs was obtained 16.79 % which was acceptable in comparison with quantum yield range (3.4 %–23.6 %) of previous studies [21,46–48].

The characterization of MSA-capped CdTe QDs with emission at red spectral region (600 nm) was done in our previous study [39]. The fluorescence spectra of MSA-capped CdTe QDs and Si@CdTe QDs are shown in Fig. 2
(A). The elimination of surface defects and passivation on QDs leading to blue shift of emission peak position of before (600 nm) and after (540 nm) silanization [49]. Elemental analysis of Si@CdTe QDs by EDX (Fig. 2C) showed that the main element was Si demonstrating successful coating of the silicate polymer around the CdTe QDs. The evolution of the functional groups of Si@CdTe QDs by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2D) revealed that the stretching vibration peak at 1058.7 cm−1 is attributed to asymmetric vibrations of (O-Si-O) and the stretching peak at 786 cm−1 are attributed to symmetric vibrations of (O-Si-O). It is necessary to mention that the bending vibration peak of NH amide was 1560.3 cm−1 and the stretching vibration peak of (CO) and (OH) were 1638.2 cm−1 and 3167.3 cm−1.

In order to optimization of ratiometric detection of DOX and DAN, different conditions such as concentrations of CDs, incubation time and PH of reaction were optimized. For this reason, the intensity changes of different concentrations of CDs (7, 15, 30, and 60 mgL−1) were studied in the presence of (2718.8 nmolL−1 and 5172 nmolL−1) DAN and DOX. It is necessary to mention that F0 = (450/550) and F = (450/550) ratio belonged to the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (450 nm) and Si@CdTe QDs (550 nm) nanocomposite, which considered for the absence and presence of drugs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 (A) variations of (F0-F)/F0 reached a plateau in 30 mgL−1 of CDs under condition of pH 7 and 30 min incubation time. So due to the best sensitivity at 30 mgL−1, this concentration was considered as an optimized concentration for further tests.

The optimization of pH was done by evaluation of RF sensor operation in PBS (0.01 mmol/L) as reaction media. Variations of fluorescence intensity for the RF sensor was probed over the pH range of 6.5–8 under condition of 30 mgL−1 CDs, and 30 min incubation time. As shown in Fig. 3 (B) variations reached to maximum at pH 7 demonstrating the reaction between drugs and CDs completed. As a result, PH 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for the detection of DOX and DAN.

Reaction time as a main effective factor on the fluorescence intensity was surveyed. The effect of incubation time was examined at 5 min intervals during one hour under condition of 30 mgL−1 CDs, and pH 7. The results revealed (Fig. 3C) that the maximum reduction of emission intensity of CDs was obtained during 40 min demonstrating reaction was completed at 40 min. So 40 min was considered as the best optimization time for incubation.

Under the beforementioned optimal condition, the introduced RF sensor was used for detection of increasing concentration of DOX and DAN. The addition of various concentrations of DOX ( Fig. 3 D) and DAN ( Fig. 3 F) led to a decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs while fluorescence intensity of Si@CdTe QDs was constant. The quenchening of the blue emission of CDs solution is shown after addtion of DOX (Fig. 3 D inset) and DAN (Fig. 3 F inset) which is in accordance with literature [50]. The calibration curves based on variations of (F0-F)/F0 versus logarithmic concentration of drugs were linear in the range of 86.2–17242 nmolL−1 (y = 0.1608x-0.271, R2 = 0.997) for DOX ( Fig. 3 E) with LOD of 29 nmolL−1 and 54.37–13594.34 nmolL−1 (y = 0.1494x-0.1063, R2 = 0.998) for DAN ( Fig. 3 G) with LOD of 18.53 nmolL−1. The limit of quantification (LOQ) values for DAN and DOX were 54.37 nmolL−1 (0.045 µgmL−1) and 86.2 nmolL−1 (0.046 µgmL−1), respectively, which were acceptable for detection of normal level of DAN and DOX in blood after usage of free drugs (1–2 µgmL−1) and liposomal encapsulation (40 µgmL−1) [26]. Based on summarized data mentioned in Table 1 , it was obviously demonstrated that the sensitivity of the introduced ratiometric sensor is acceptable in comparison with other studies. It should be noted that the intra-day and inter-day (RSD%, n = 3) repeatability values for detection of 862 nM of DOX were 2.31 % and 3.96 %, respectively. Moreover, these values for detection of DAN were 2.08 % and 4.7 %, respectively.

After incubation of drugs with CDs and Si@CdTe QDs system, the fluorescence intesity at 450 nm was quenched, while the intesity at 550 nm was constant. In this study for preventing the effect of drugs on the Si@CdTe QDs intesity and performance of ratiometric method, MSA-CdTe QDs were covered by silica layer. This event prevents direct interaction of anthraquinone drugs with MSA-CdTe QDs due to repulse of positive-charged amine groups of silica shell and positive-charged anthraquinone drugs leading to prevention of electron transfer from QDs to quinones and providing trustworthy reference signal for perfomance of ratiometric method [25,61]. On the other side, the anthraquinone drugs of DOX and DAN can be considrably adsorbed on the negatively-charged CDs through electrostatic interaction followed by quenching of fluorescence intensity. As presented in Fig. 3 (H) fluorescence spectrum of CDs at 450 nm cosidrably overlaped with absorbtion spectra of DOX and DAN at 490 nm. Inner filter effect (IFE) is related to an event that the emission/excitation light of a fluorophore can be absorbed by an absorber due to a spectral overlaping between them [55]. So, the most possible mechanism for quenching of CDs fluorescence intensity by DOX and DAN is IFE phenomena.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the introduced ratiometric method and evaluation of matrix effect on the analysis of DAN and DOX, the middle and final concentrations of drugs in the linear range were chosen to make considerable changes in intensity. For this purpose after spiking the pretreated serum with some concentrations of drugs (Table 2 ), the samples were incubated with CDs and Si@CdTeQDs for 40 min. Afterwards the detection was done by proposed RF sensor and recoveries were obtained using calibration curve. The result of the investigation (Table 2) showed the applicability of the introduced RF sensor for the detection of DOX and DAN with a recovery of 86.32 %-97.53 % and 86.14 %-99.62 %, respectively. Moreover RSD (n = 3) for detection of DAN and DOX were 3.38 %-1.48 % and 3 %-1.47 %, respectively.

To identify the effect of the different interferes, which may present in the biological sample, on detection of 0.3 mgL−1 of drugs (0.286 mgL−1 DAN 543.77 nmolL−1and DOX 551.95 nmolL−1), the tolerance level of 5±% was considered as the limited range for changing the intensity of sensor signals [62]. As presented in Fig. 4 (A and B), the difference matrices containing K2CO3 (250 mgL−1 for DAN and 400 mgL−1 for DOX), Glutamine 250 mgL−1, KNO3 500 mgL−1, Al2(SO4)3 18H2O 500 mgL−1, Fructose 500 mgL−1, Glucose 500 mgL−1, MgCl2·6H2O 500 mgL−1, CaCl2 500 mgL−1, NaCl (500 mgL−1 for DAN and 250 mgL−1 for DOX), CuSO4 500 mgL−1, Na2HPO4 (500 mgL−1 DAN and 250 mgL−1 DOX), KCl 500 mgL−1, Tryptophane 250 mgL−1, Methionine 250 mgL−1, Dopamine 250 mgL−1, and Histidine 250 mgL−1 did not cause significant changes (< ±5%) in signal intensity. The maximum error percentage for detection of DAN and DOX was in the presence of Histidine 250 mgL−1 (5 %) and K2CO3 400 mgL−1 (4.95 %), respectively. These results demonstrated the superb efficiency of proposed RF sensor for analysis of DAN and DOX.

The MTT assay as a colorimetric method was used to assess nanoparticles cell metabolic activity and cytotoxicity [63]. The base of this test is the conversion of yellow tetrazolium dye MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) to insoluble formazan ((E,Z)-5-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-1,3-diphenylformazan) by NAD(P)H-dependent cellular oxidoreductase enzyme [64]. The concentrations of 5–1500 mgL−1 CDs were incubated 24 h with MCF-7 and PC3 cell lines, and according to results (Fig. 4C), minimum toxicity was observed on cell lines. Also, some in-vivo and in-vitro studies indicated that CDs have not negative effect on the biological parameters at reasonable concentration and can be considred as a safe and harmless alternative for biological uses in the future [65,66]. So, according to the results (Fig. 4 C) 600 mgL−1 was selected for imaging analysis.

Up to now living cells have been detected by fluorescent dyes and imaging techniques, while many of these dyes are toxic and cause cell death, or their fluorescence is weak and should be used in high concentrations [67]. So, the development of easy to synthesis and low toxicity agents is highly important for imaging. Because of their small size and unique property, CDs can quickly enter the cells and remained in them [68]. For this purpose CDs (600 mgL−1) with high safety were used as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 4 (D and E) CDs were observed in the cell membrane and cytoplasmic region without any damage to the cell nucleus [69], and the cells were easily imaged.

In this study, bell pepper-synthesized CDs and Si@CdTe QDs were successfully used for designing dual emission RF and detection of DAN and DOX in human samples. In presence of anthraquinone drugs the intensity of CDs was quenched, while the intesity of Si@CdTe QDs was constant. So, a sensetive RF method was conducted to concidrablely detect DAN and DOX in serum sample whithot any significant interferences. The notable benefits such as eco-friendly, fast response time, high selectivity and sesitivity, good precision and wide-ranging response, condidate preposed RF sesnor as a poetent technique for detection of anthraquinone drugs in biological samples. Furthermore imaging of two important cell lines including PC3 and MCF-7 was successfully performed by green-synthesized CDs. So, green and economical CDs can be a powerful altenative for commercial fluorescent dyes. Therefore, we can suggest utilization of green synthesized CDs for the biological imaging and detection.

Arash Mohammadinejad: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Khalil Abnous: Supervision, Resources. Morteza Alinezhad Nameghi: Validation, Software. Roghayeh Yahyazadeh: Writing – original draft. Sara Hamrah: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Fereshteh Senobari: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Seyed Ahmad Mohajeri: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Vice Chancellor of Research of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran.
This study supported by Vice Chancellor of Research of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran, through the grant numbers 971785 and 971760.
